title: practical english usage - Part 240
content:
Dan's the family conscience, so to speak. He's always telling us what's right.

’ I've heard there's been a fire at the office.' ’ Yes, apparently so.' A lot of people say he's a bad manager. In my view/opinion, he's OK.

(►   359.2);             (►   358); of; really; well, at least; I'm afraid   actually   or rather; that is to say; more or less; I think/feel/suppose/reckon/guess; sort of; kind softening                and       correcting: in my view/opinion; apparently; so to speak; I guess, looking back on it, I was her toy boy.

I've got a fu nny feeling about him. In other words /I mean, I don't trust him.

There's a problem about tomorrow. That is to say, I don't think we can come.

making             things           clear: that is to say; in other words; I mean; I guess ’ What do you think of my hair? ’ Frankly, darling it's a disaster.' Honestly, I think your poetry is remarkable.

(No doubt is often ironic: the speaker may not be at all sure of Mr  Jenkins' No doubt you will be paying your rent by the end of the week, Mr Jenkins?

showing              speaker's               attitude: no doubt; honestly; frankly or modify what is being said to make the communication more effective.

Some discourse markers express the speaker's attitude to what he/she is saying, 3 attitude For other uses of still, ►    595.      For a comparison of on the contrary and on the other hand, ►    428.1 .

Jess and Simon.

We could invite some of the neighbours, for instance /for example, out what Joshua thinks.

I'd like to have time to talk to some of the others. In particular, I want to find giving           examples: in particular; for instance; for example she's going to meet. And besides / I mean, she's far too young.

I don't like her going out alone at night. You don't know what sort of people really friendly.

The place is close to the sea. And another thing is, the people are putting up VAT.

They're cutting unemployment benefits. And on top of that, they're he says he has.

I don't like his attitude. Also, I don't believe he's actually got the qualifications (►   526.4) I mean adding: also; on top of that; as well as that; another thing is; besides; It's a bit expensive, don't you think?

You're taking this too seriously, aren't you?

We can't go to the gym now. Look, they close in an hour.

A  speaker says 'you know' to tell the hearer (or to pretend) that they share the (►   540); you see checking               common               ground: you know; (you) know what I mean?; of course I mean, things are kind of tight just now,.. .' 'When can you pay me back? 'Well, you know, let me think, I don't know, And.. .' 'How much  is it going to cost? 'Let me see. There's materials. And transport.

know; I mean; kind of; sort of gaining             time: let me see; let's see; let me think; well; you know; I don't 'How did the team do? 'Well, they lost.' 'Was the skiing good? 'Yes, actually, it was wonderful.* 'Hello, Adrian.' 'Actually, my name's Richard.' out for a year and get a job.' 'How are your medical studies going? 'As a matter of fact, I've decided to drop everyone was saying.' 'So what do you think? 'To tell the truth, I wasn't really listening to what perhaps confirm what the hearer or reader thinks.

These expressions introduce information that will contradict (usually) or to tell the truth; well; actually referring               to    the      hearer's             expectations: in fact; actually; as a matter of fact; certainly be there before 8.30.

I'm not sure what time I'll arrive, maybe seven or eight. In any case, I'll better than sleeping in the car.

The hotel's probably OK. It's cheap, and the place seems clean. At any rate, it's I can't get to the hospital to see Julie. I mean, not this week, anyhow.
